title: Conditionals — Band 7+ Reference
subtitle: Zero / 1st / 2nd / 3rd + Mixed + Inverted + Wish/If only — the highest Band 7+ signal
intro: Conditionals are gold for IELTS Band 7+. They show complex thinking — cause-effect, hypothesis, regret, hypothetical reasoning. Speaking Part 3 examiners specifically use 'What would happen if…' to test conditional control. Mixed and inverted conditionals are the single clearest Band 7.5+ markers in Writing Task 2.

sections.title: 1. The Four Standard Conditionals
sections.summary: Memorise the form of each. Choosing the wrong one for the meaning is a Band 6 grammar error.

rules.pattern: ZERO CONDITIONAL — general truths / scientific facts

examples.plural: If you heat water to 100°C, it boils.
If people exercise regularly, they live longer.
If I drink coffee at night, I can't sleep.

rules.note: 'When' often replaces 'if' here, with the same meaning: 'When you heat water…'

rules.pattern: FIRST CONDITIONAL — likely / possible future

examples.plural: If it rains tomorrow, we will cancel the trip.
If you don't study, you will fail the exam.
If you help me, I will help you in return.

rules.note: Modal alternatives: 'will' can be replaced by 'can / may / might / should': 'If it rains, we MAY stay home.'

rules.pattern: SECOND CONDITIONAL — unlikely / imaginary present or future

examples.singular: Form: If + Past Simple, would + V
examples.plural: If I had more time, I would learn another language.
If I were you, I would accept the offer.
If everyone used public transport, cities would be cleaner.

rules.note: Use 'were' (not 'was') for all persons in formal English: 'If I WERE rich…' This is the subjunctive form examiners reward.

rules.pattern: THIRD CONDITIONAL — impossible past (regret / hypothetical past)

examples.singular: Form: If + Past Perfect, would have + V3
examples.plural: If I had studied medicine, I would have become a doctor.
If she had left earlier, she would not have missed the train.
If governments had acted sooner, the crisis would have been avoided.

rules.note: ❌ Common error: using 'would have' in the if-clause: 'If I would have studied…' This is wrong. Use Past Perfect ('If I had studied…').

sections.title: 2. Mixed Conditionals — The Band 7+ Signature
sections.summary: Mixed conditionals combine times. Using one accurate mixed conditional in a Task 2 essay is a strong Band 7.5+ marker — most candidates never reach this.

rules.pattern: TYPE A — past condition → present result

examples.singular: Form: If + Past Perfect, would + V
examples.plural: If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.
If she had taken the job in London, she would be living there now.
If governments had invested in renewable energy in the 1990s, climate change would not be such a serious problem today.

rules.note: The action in the if-clause happened (or didn't) in the past; the result applies right now.

rules.pattern: TYPE B — present condition → past result

examples.singular: Form: If + Past Simple, would have + V3
examples.plural: If I were better organised, I would not have missed the deadline.
If he weren't so impatient, he would have got the promotion.
If she didn't speak French, she would not have got the job in Paris.

rules.note: The if-clause describes a general or ongoing condition (true in present); the result clause refers to a specific past event.

sections.tip: Memorise ONE template per type and deploy it under exam pressure: 'If I hadn't [past action], I wouldn't be [present state] today.' (Type A) — perfect for Speaking Part 2 about turning points.

sections.title: 3. Inverted Conditionals (Drop 'if')
sections.summary: Drop 'if' and invert subject and auxiliary. Formal, written register. One per essay is impressive; three sounds forced.

rules.pattern: FIRST CONDITIONAL inversion — Should + Subject + V

examples.singular: If you should need help → Should you need help
examples.plural: Should you need any further information, please contact me.
Should the situation arise, the committee will reconvene.

rules.note: Common in formal letters and reports.

rules.pattern: SECOND CONDITIONAL inversion — Were + Subject + (to) + V

examples.singular: If I were to choose → Were I to choose
examples.plural: Were I to choose again, I would pick a different career.
Were governments to act decisively, climate change could be mitigated.

examples.singular: If it were not for + noun
examples.plural: Were it not for technology, modern life would be unrecognisable.

rules.pattern: THIRD CONDITIONAL inversion — Had + Subject + V3

examples.singular: If I had known → Had I known
examples.plural: Had I known the risks, I would never have invested.
Had she not intervened, the situation would have escalated.

examples.singular: If it had not been for + noun
examples.plural: Had it not been for the new policy, the crisis would have continued.

sections.tip: In Task 2 conclusions, drop in ONE inverted conditional: 'Should this trend continue…' or 'Were governments to take stronger action…'. That single sentence raises the perceived register of the whole conclusion.

sections.title: 4. Wish / If only / I'd rather / It's (high) time
sections.summary: All four use the UNREAL PAST (Past Simple or Past Perfect) even when the meaning is present or future. This is one of the highest-yield Band 7+ patterns.

rules.pattern: WISH / IF ONLY + Past Simple — present unreality (something not true now)

examples.plural: I wish I knew the answer.
She wishes she spoke French.
If only it weren't raining.

rules.note: Use 'were' (not 'was') for formal register: 'I wish I WERE taller.'

rules.pattern: WISH / IF ONLY + Past Perfect — past regret

examples.plural: I wish I had studied harder.
If only we hadn't taken that route.
Many citizens wish the government had acted sooner.

rules.pattern: WISH + would — complaint about someone else's behaviour

examples.plural: I wish he would stop interrupting.
I wish it would stop raining.

rules.note: Don't use 'wish + would' for yourself: ❌ 'I wish I would study harder.' Use 'I wish I studied harder' (Simple) or 'could' (I wish I could study harder).

rules.pattern: I'D RATHER + Subject + Past Simple — preference about now

examples.plural: I'd rather you didn't smoke here.
She'd rather we left earlier.

rules.note: If the subject is the SAME as the speaker, use bare infinitive: 'I'd rather stay at home' (no 'past', no 'to').

rules.pattern: IT'S (HIGH) TIME + Subject + Past Simple — something should be happening NOW

examples.plural: It's high time the government acted on climate change.
It's time you found a job.
It's about time someone said something.

rules.note: The Past Simple form is misleading — the meaning is PRESENT/FUTURE urgency, not past.

sections.tip: Speaking Part 3 questions about social problems are perfect for 'It's high time + Past Simple': 'It's high time governments invested more in education / regulated social media / addressed inequality.'

sections.title: 5. Alternatives to 'if'
sections.summary: Vary your conditional connectors. Repeating 'if-if-if' across an essay sounds Band 6.

rules.pattern: PROVIDED THAT / PROVIDING / AS LONG AS — on the condition that

examples.plural: Students may attend the workshop, provided that they register in advance.
I'll lend you the book, as long as you return it next week.

rules.pattern: UNLESS — if not (used with positive verb)

examples.plural: Unless you study, you will fail.
We will go to the park unless it rains.

rules.note: ❌ Common error: 'Unless you don't study, you will fail.' 'Unless' already contains the negative — never pair it with another negative.

rules.pattern: SUPPOSING / SUPPOSE / WHAT IF — hypothetical (often spoken)

examples.plural: Supposing you won the lottery, what would you do?
What if everyone worked from home permanently?

rules.pattern: IN CASE — as a precaution (NOT 'if')

examples.plural: Take an umbrella in case it rains.
Save your work regularly in case the computer crashes.

rules.note: 'In case' ≠ 'if'. 'In case it rains' means 'so that you are prepared if it does'. 'If it rains' means 'on condition that it rains'.

rules.pattern: ON CONDITION THAT / ASSUMING THAT — formal alternatives

examples.plural: The funding will be granted on condition that progress reports are submitted quarterly.
Assuming current trends continue, global temperatures will rise by 2°C by 2050.

rules.pattern: OTHERWISE — implicit conditional ('if not')

examples.plural: Wear a coat; otherwise, you will catch a cold. (= if you don't wear a coat)
Submit the application by Friday; otherwise, your place may be withdrawn.

sections.title: 6. Conditionals in IELTS Speaking & Writing
sections.summary: Examiners specifically design questions to test conditional control. Recognise the prompt and deploy the right form.

rules.pattern: Speaking Part 3 — hypothetical questions trigger Second Conditional

examples.singular: 'What would happen if everyone used public transport?'
examples.plural: If everyone used public transport, cities would be cleaner and traffic would be reduced.

examples.singular: 'How would things change if working hours were shorter?'
examples.plural: If working hours were shorter, employees would be more productive and have better work-life balance.

rules.note: A Band 6 candidate answers in Present Simple ('Cities are cleaner…'). A Band 7+ candidate stays in 'would' throughout the answer.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — using Mixed conditionals for cause-effect

examples.plural: If governments had invested in renewable energy decades ago, we would not be facing such a serious climate crisis today.
If schools had introduced coding earlier, today's workforce would be far better prepared for technological change.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — Inverted conditionals in formal tone

examples.plural: Were governments to invest more in public transport, urban congestion would be significantly reduced.
Should this trend continue, the consequences for future generations will be severe.

rules.pattern: Writing Task 2 — 'It's high time' for criticism / urgency

examples.plural: It is high time policymakers acknowledged the scale of the problem.
It is about time society took collective responsibility for environmental protection.

sections.tip: Plan to use AT LEAST 3 different conditional forms in any 250-word Task 2 essay: one Second, one Third or Mixed, and one alternative form (unless / provided that / inverted).

sections.title: 7. Top Conditional Errors at the Band 6 → 7+ Threshold
sections.summary: These specific errors pull a Band 7 candidate down to Band 6.5. Each is fixable with conscious practice.

rules.pattern: 1. 'Would' inside the if-clause

examples.singular: ❌ If I would have time, I would help.
examples.plural: ✔ If I had time, I would help. (Second Conditional)

examples.singular: ❌ If I would have known, I would have called.
examples.plural: ✔ If I had known, I would have called. (Third Conditional)

rules.note: Rule: 'would' goes in the RESULT clause, never in the if-clause (with rare exceptions for politeness).

rules.pattern: 2. 'Will' inside the if-clause (mixing First and Zero)

examples.singular: ❌ If it will rain tomorrow, we will stay home.
examples.plural: ✔ If it rains tomorrow, we will stay home.

rules.pattern: 3. Double negative with 'unless'

examples.singular: ❌ Unless you don't try, you will fail.
examples.plural: ✔ Unless you try, you will fail.

rules.pattern: 4. 'Was' instead of 'were' in formal Second Conditional

examples.singular: ❌ If I was you, I would accept.
examples.plural: ✔ If I were you, I would accept.

rules.note: 'Was' is acceptable in informal speech, but Band 7+ writing should use 'were' for all persons.

rules.pattern: 5. Mixing tenses across the conditional incorrectly

examples.singular: ❌ If I had studied harder, I would get a better job now (Third in if-clause + present 'now' in result).
examples.plural: ✔ If I had studied harder, I would HAVE A better job now. (Mixed Type A — past condition + present result)

examples.singular: ❌ If I would study harder, my marks would improve.
examples.plural: ✔ If I studied harder, my marks would improve. (consistent Second Conditional)

rules.note: Pick a conditional type and stay in it — or use a Mixed Conditional deliberately. Don't mix accidentally.

rules.pattern: 6. 'In case' confused with 'if'

examples.singular: ❌ Take an umbrella if it rains.
examples.plural: ✔ Take an umbrella in case it rains. (= as a precaution, before knowing if it rains)

rules.note: 'If' = on condition that. 'In case' = as a precaution against the possibility of.

rules.pattern: 7. Inversion errors (forgetting subject-auxiliary swap)

examples.singular: ❌ Should you would need help, contact me.
examples.plural: ✔ Should you need help, contact me.

examples.singular: ❌ Were I to known the answer, I would tell you.
examples.plural: ✔ Were I to know the answer, I would tell you. (use base form after 'to')

rules.pattern: 8. Forgetting tense shift in 'wish'

examples.singular: ❌ I wish I have more time.
examples.plural: ✔ I wish I had more time.

examples.singular: ❌ I wish I would be taller.
examples.plural: ✔ I wish I were taller. (use Past Simple for present unreality, not 'would')

sections.tip: Self-test: write 8 sentences (one per conditional type + alternatives) on a real Band-7 topic like climate change or education. Check each against this error list before counting them as 'mastered'.

checklist: I can name and form Zero, First, Second, Third — and write one accurate example of each.
I never put 'would' or 'will' inside an if-clause in standard conditionals.
I use 'were' (not 'was') for all persons in formal Second Conditional and 'wish' constructions.
I can produce both types of Mixed Conditional — past condition → present result, and present condition → past result.
I can deploy at least one Inverted Conditional (Should you / Were I to / Had I known) in a Task 2 essay without it sounding forced.
I use Wish + Past Simple for present unreality and Wish + Past Perfect for past regret — and never mix the two.
I use 'unless' with a positive verb and never write a double negative.
I can answer Speaking Part 3 hypothetical questions (What would happen if…) staying in the 'would' world throughout.
I use 'It's high time + Past Simple' for present urgency in social-issues essays.
I plan to deploy at least 3 different conditional forms in any Band 7+ Task 2 essay.
